heuristics.rule: Apply for PGWP as soon as you receive your letter of completion — do not wait for your diploma, certificate, or convocation ceremony.
heuristics.condition: When you have just finished your final coursework or placement and are waiting for official graduation documents
heuristics.action: Submit your PGWP application online within 180 days of receiving your letter of completion, using that letter plus unofficial transcript as proof
heuristics.because: The 180-day clock starts from the letter of completion date, not convocation. Waiting for a diploma wastes your window and risks missing the deadline entirely.

heuristics.rule: Submit your PGWP application before your study permit expires, not after — even a single day of expired status can disqualify you.
heuristics.condition: When your study permit expiry date is approaching and you have not yet graduated or applied for PGWP
heuristics.action: Confirm your study permit is valid at the time of PGWP submission; if it has expired, consult an RCIC immediately about restoration options
heuristics.because: IRCC requires your study permit to have been valid at some point during the 180 days after graduation AND at the time of application — both conditions must be met.

heuristics.rule: If you submitted your study permit application on or after November 1, 2024, verify your program's CIP code against IRCC's eligible fields list before applying for PGWP.
heuristics.condition: When you are a post-November 2024 study permit holder preparing to apply for PGWP
heuristics.action: Cross-check your NSCC program's CIP code against IRCC's current eligible fields list (agriculture, healthcare, STEM, trades, transport) before submitting
heuristics.because: Field-of-study eligibility is now a hard requirement for this cohort — applying without confirming eligibility wastes the $255 fee and risks a denial that ends your legal status.

heuristics.rule: Do not travel outside Canada after submitting your PGWP application unless you have a valid TRV or eTA in your passport.
heuristics.condition: When your PGWP application is pending and you are considering international travel
heuristics.action: Check your TRV/eTA validity before booking travel; if expired, wait until your PGWP is approved before renewing, since TRV renewal requires new status
heuristics.because: You cannot renew a TRV while in PGWP-pending limbo; without a valid TRV or eTA you will be unable to re-enter Canada, stranding you outside the country.

heuristics.rule: Select 'No' for the job offer question during the PGWP application questionnaire even if you already have a job lined up.
heuristics.condition: When completing the IRCC online questionnaire for an open work permit / PGWP
heuristics.action: Answer 'No' to 'Do you have a written job offer?' to correctly route your application to an open work permit rather than an employer-specific permit
heuristics.because: Answering 'Yes' routes you to a closed, employer-specific work permit — losing the open-work-permit flexibility that is the entire value of the PGWP.

heuristics.rule: Upload your language test results under 'Client Information,' not under 'Proof of Language Proficiency,' because no such dedicated field exists in the IRCC portal.
heuristics.condition: When uploading documents for a PGWP application in the IRCC online system
heuristics.action: Place CELPIP/IELTS/PTE Core results in the 'Client Information' document upload section
heuristics.because: Students who look for a dedicated language proficiency field and find none may skip uploading their scores entirely, causing application refusal for missing evidence.

heuristics.rule: Once you submit your PGWP application with a valid study permit and qualifying program history, begin working full-time immediately — you do not need to wait for the permit to arrive.
heuristics.condition: When your study permit was valid at submission, your program was full-time and longer than eight months, and you did not violate your work-hour limits as a student
heuristics.action: Start full-time work as soon as the PGWP application is submitted and confirmed received by IRCC
heuristics.because: IRCC allows 'maintained status' work authorization during processing, but only if all three qualifying criteria are met — missing any one of them means you are not authorized to work and could face removal.

heuristics.rule: Ensure your language test results are no older than 2 years at the time of PGWP application.
heuristics.condition: When you took an approved language test (CELPIP, IELTS General, PTE Core) more than 18 months ago and are approaching graduation
heuristics.action: Retake the test before submitting your PGWP application if results will be over 2 years old at submission date
heuristics.because: Expired language test scores are treated as if no test was submitted — the application will be refused regardless of your actual English proficiency.

decision_factors.factor: Study permit application date relative to November 1, 2024
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Students who applied for their study permit on or after November 1, 2024 face an additional field-of-study eligibility requirement that earlier cohorts do not; this can render a completed degree worthless for PGWP purposes
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Check the original study permit application submission date (not approval date) against November 1, 2024; then verify the program's CIP code against IRCC's current eligible fields list

decision_factors.factor: Study permit validity at time of PGWP application
decision_factors.why_it_matters: A lapsed study permit at the moment of submission is a hard disqualifier — there is no grace period
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Compare study permit expiry date against your intended application submission date; flag any gap greater than zero days as a critical risk requiring legal advice

decision_factors.factor: Full-time enrollment status throughout the program
decision_factors.why_it_matters: A single semester (other than the final term) where you were enrolled part-time can disqualify you from PGWP entirely
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Review official enrollment history with the registrar for every semester; flag any semester listed as part-time and consult an RCIC before applying

decision_factors.why_it_matters: Minimum CLB 5 is required for college graduates; scores older than 2 years are rejected regardless of score level
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Confirm test score meets CLB 5 equivalency for the specific test taken (CELPIP, IELTS General, or PTE Core) and that the test date is within 2 years of the intended PGWP submission date

decision_factors.factor: Whether the student worked more than authorized hours during studies
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Exceeding authorized work hours as a student disqualifies you from the 'work while you wait' provision — meaning you cannot legally work during PGWP processing even after submitting
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Review pay stubs, T4s, or employer records against the 20-hour weekly limit that applied while school was in session; flag any weeks of overwork

decision_factors.factor: Spouse's occupational TEER level and PGWP remaining validity
decision_factors.why_it_matters: As of January 21, 2025, spousal open work permits are only available if the PGWP holder works in TEER 0/1 or select TEER 2/3 roles AND has at least 16 months remaining on the permit
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Look up the NOC code of the PGWP holder's job and confirm TEER classification; calculate months remaining on PGWP at the time the spouse would apply

decision_factors.factor: Medical exam history and intended occupation
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Certain occupations (healthcare, childcare, agricultural work) and prior residence in designated countries trigger mandatory medical exam requirements that add cost and time to the application
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Check IRCC's designated country list and intended job duties against the public-health-contact criteria; budget extra time and $85+ in biometric fees if applicable

decision_factors.factor: TRV or eTA validity for re-entry after travel
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Traveling while PGWP is pending without a valid TRV or eTA means you cannot legally re-enter Canada until PGWP is approved — potentially months of being stranded abroad
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Check passport for current TRV or eTA expiry before booking any international travel post-PGWP submission

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Waiting until convocation or diploma receipt to start PGWP application
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Significant portion of the 180-day application window is silently consumed, increasing risk of missing the deadline if any document delays occur; also delays work authorization start date
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Students who assume the diploma is required before applying — a very common misunderstanding for first-generation international students

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Traveling internationally while PGWP application is pending
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: If TRV or eTA has expired, you can re-enter Canada only after PGWP is approved; PGWP processing times are variable and you may be stuck outside Canada for weeks or months with no work authorization
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Students who travel home for family visits immediately after graduation without checking re-entry document validity

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Applying for PGWP without verifying field-of-study CIP code eligibility (post-Nov 2024 cohort)
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: A PGWP denial immediately terminates legal status in Canada — you must stop working and are required to leave the country; there is no automatic right to remain as a visitor
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Post-November 2024 study permit holders in programs that may not map to IRCC's five eligible occupational categories

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Working full-time while PGWP is pending without confirming all three qualifying criteria are met
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: If any one of the three criteria (valid study permit at submission, full-time program over 8 months, no work-hour violations as a student) is not met, working during the pending period is unauthorized — creating an inadmissibility record that can affect future immigration applications
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Students who worked over 20 hours per week during busy academic periods and assume the 'work while you wait' rule applies to them automatically

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Enrolling as a part-time student in any non-final semester to manage workload or personal circumstances
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: A single part-time semester (except the final semester) silently disqualifies you from the entire PGWP, regardless of how many full-time semesters preceded it
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Students who reduce course load temporarily due to health, family, or work pressures without understanding the PGWP implications

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Assuming a PGWP holder's spouse automatically qualifies for an open work permit
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Post-January 2025 rules restrict spousal OWPs to specific TEER levels and require the PGWP holder to have 16+ months remaining — many spouses who previously qualified no longer do under the new rules
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Families where the PGWP holder works in TEER 2-4 occupations not on the shortage list, or where the PGWP has less than 16 months remaining

failure_patterns.pattern: Submitting the PGWP application after the 180-day window has closed
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students conflate the 180-day clock starting from graduation ceremony or diploma receipt rather than the letter of completion; some wait for 'official' documents that are not required
failure_patterns.consequence: Permanent loss of PGWP eligibility for that program — no appeals or extensions; must leave Canada or apply for a different status
warning_signs: Student says 'I'm waiting for my diploma to arrive before applying'
Student graduated more than 4 months ago and has not yet applied
Student is unsure when their letter of completion was issued

failure_patterns.pattern: Selecting 'Yes' to the job offer question during the IRCC questionnaire, resulting in a closed work permit application instead of an open PGWP
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: The questionnaire wording is counterintuitive — students with jobs think honesty requires them to say yes, not understanding that 'yes' routes to an employer-specific permit
failure_patterns.consequence: Student receives a closed work permit tied to one employer instead of an open PGWP; loses the flexibility to change jobs freely and may need to restart the application process
warning_signs: Student has already accepted a job offer before applying for PGWP
Student did not use an RCIC or consult the school's step-by-step guide before starting the questionnaire

failure_patterns.pattern: Assuming any Canadian college program qualifies for PGWP without checking the CIP code (post-November 2024 cohort)
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students and even some advisors are unaware of the field-of-study eligibility change that took effect November 1, 2024; the rule only applies to newer study permit holders, creating a two-tier system that is easy to misapply
failure_patterns.consequence: PGWP denial, immediate loss of legal status, required departure from Canada, potential bar on future applications depending on actions taken post-denial
warning_signs: Student applied for study permit after November 2024 and is in a business, arts, or social science program
Student has not verified their program's CIP code against IRCC's eligible list

failure_patterns.pattern: Failing to upload language test scores because no dedicated field is visible in the IRCC portal
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: The IRCC portal does not label a specific field 'Proof of Language Proficiency' — students search for it, do not find it, and either skip it or contact IRCC assuming it is not required
failure_patterns.consequence: Application refused for missing mandatory evidence; $255 fee lost; student must refile and loses work authorization time
warning_signs: Student says 'I couldn't find where to upload my IELTS scores'
Student submitted application without uploading any language document

failure_patterns.pattern: Traveling internationally after PGWP submission without a valid TRV or eTA, then being unable to re-enter Canada
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students know they submitted their application and assume 'pending status' allows normal international travel; they do not realize TRV/eTA renewal is impossible until PGWP is approved
failure_patterns.consequence: Student is stranded outside Canada, loses employment, potentially loses housing, and cannot return until PGWP is approved — a process that can take months
warning_signs: Student's TRV or eTA expires within the next 3-6 months
Student is planning international travel immediately after graduation

failure_patterns.pattern: Working full-time during PGWP processing without meeting all three eligibility criteria for 'work while you wait'
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students hear that they can work while waiting and assume the rule applies universally, without checking whether they violated work-hour limits during studies or had a part-time enrollment period
failure_patterns.consequence: Unauthorized work record on immigration file; potential inadmissibility finding; jeopardizes future permanent residence applications through Express Entry or PNP
warning_signs: Student worked more than 20 hours in any single week during the academic semester
Student had a semester of part-time enrollment
Student's study permit had a gap or expired briefly at any point

diagnostic_questions.question: What date did you submit your original study permit application — before or after November 1, 2024?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the field-of-study CIP code eligibility requirement applies to this student, which is a hard additional gate for PGWP eligibility
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: What is your program's CIP code, and is it on IRCC's current list of eligible fields linked to agriculture, healthcare, STEM, trades, or transport?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: You are not subject to the field-of-study restriction; proceed to verify the other eligibility criteria (full-time status, study permit validity, language test)

diagnostic_questions.question: Were you enrolled as a full-time student in every semester of your program except the final term?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether there is a hidden PGWP disqualification due to a part-time semester that the student may not even remember or understand as problematic
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Confirm with the registrar's office that each semester's enrollment record officially shows full-time status before submitting
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Immediately consult an RCIC — a part-time semester (other than the final term) disqualifies you from PGWP, and you need to explore alternative pathways before applying

diagnostic_questions.question: Is your study permit currently valid, and will it still be valid on the date you plan to submit the PGWP application?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the student faces a hard eligibility barrier that cannot be overcome by simply filing the application quickly
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Proceed with application preparation; confirm the exact expiry date and build in a buffer for any document-gathering delays
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Contact an RCIC immediately to assess whether status restoration is possible; do not submit a PGWP application with an expired study permit without legal advice

diagnostic_questions.question: Did you at any point work more than 20 hours per week during a semester when school was in session?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the student qualifies for 'work while you wait' authorization during PGWP processing — unauthorized work during pending status creates an inadmissibility record
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Do not begin working after submitting the PGWP application until you receive the actual permit; consult an RCIC about whether to disclose the prior work violation in the application
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: You likely qualify for maintained-status work authorization during processing — confirm the other two criteria (valid study permit at submission, full-time program over 8 months) are also met

diagnostic_questions.question: When was your language proficiency test taken, and will those results be less than 2 years old on your planned PGWP submission date?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the student needs to retake a language test before filing — submitting with expired scores guarantees refusal
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Confirm the test type is one of the three approved by IRCC (CELPIP General, IELTS General, PTE Core) and that the score meets CLB 5
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Book a retake immediately; factor in test registration lead time and results turnaround time against the 180-day PGWP application deadline

diagnostic_questions.question: Are you planning to travel outside Canada at any point between now and when your PGWP is approved?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the student is at risk of being stranded outside Canada due to an expired TRV or eTA during the PGWP processing period
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Check TRV and eTA validity in your passport right now — if either expires before your expected PGWP approval date, you must either cancel travel plans or accept the risk of extended time outside Canada
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: No additional action needed on this dimension; focus on ensuring other eligibility criteria are met

diagnostic_questions.question: Does your spouse intend to apply for an open work permit based on your PGWP status?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the post-January 2025 spousal OWP restrictions apply and whether the family's dual-income plan is achievable
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: What is your NOC code and TEER classification, and how many months remain on your PGWP? — both must meet new thresholds (TEER 0/1 or qualifying TEER 2/3, plus 16+ months remaining)
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: No action needed on spousal OWP; focus on primary PGWP application

industry_realities.common_belief: You need to wait for your diploma or attend convocation before you can apply for the PGWP
industry_realities.actual_reality: The PGWP application can and should be submitted as soon as you receive your letter of completion — the diploma and convocation are irrelevant to the application timeline
industry_realities.implication: Students who wait for their diploma may unnecessarily consume weeks or months of their 180-day window, increasing risk of missing the deadline if any complications arise

industry_realities.common_belief: Any Canadian college program from a designated learning institution qualifies you for a PGWP
industry_realities.actual_reality: Since November 1, 2024, students who applied for their study permit on or after that date must also have graduated from a program in one of five approved occupational fields (agriculture, healthcare, STEM, trades, transport); other programs do not qualify
industry_realities.implication: Students in business, arts, humanities, and social science programs who applied for study permits after November 2024 may have no path to PGWP regardless of graduation status — they need to investigate this before completing their program, not after

industry_realities.common_belief: Once you submit your PGWP application, you can automatically work full-time while you wait
industry_realities.actual_reality: The 'work while you wait' provision has three specific qualifying conditions — all must be met simultaneously; failing even one (e.g., having worked overtime during school) means you are not authorized to work and doing so creates an inadmissibility issue
industry_realities.implication: Students should audit their own work history before assuming work authorization, rather than starting a full-time job the day after submission

industry_realities.common_belief: A PGWP holder's spouse is automatically eligible for an open work permit
industry_realities.actual_reality: As of January 21, 2025, spousal OWPs require the PGWP holder to work in TEER 0 or 1, or select TEER 2/3 shortage occupations, AND to have at least 16 months remaining on their PGWP at the time of the spouse's application — most entry-level PGWP holders will not meet these thresholds
industry_realities.implication: Families planning finances around dual income should verify the PGWP holder's TEER classification before the spouse declines other options; many couples who qualified under old rules no longer do

industry_realities.common_belief: If your PGWP application is denied, you have some time to appeal or arrange your affairs before needing to leave Canada
industry_realities.actual_reality: A PGWP denial immediately ends your legal status in Canada — you must stop working immediately and are required to leave; there is no automatic appeal period, though you may be able to apply for visitor status restoration
industry_realities.implication: Students should treat a PGWP application with the same seriousness as a final job interview — getting it wrong has immediate, severe, and largely irreversible consequences in the short term

industry_realities.common_belief: Having a job offer before applying for PGWP is an advantage you should declare in the application
industry_realities.actual_reality: Declaring a job offer in the IRCC questionnaire routes your application toward an employer-specific closed work permit, not the open PGWP; the correct answer is 'No' to the job offer question to obtain an open permit
industry_realities.implication: Students should follow the step-by-step questionnaire guide exactly as written rather than trying to 'honestly' reflect their current situation — the system's logic is counterintuitive and correct answers are not always the most transparent-seeming ones

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Waits for convocation or diploma before initiating PGWP application; treats graduation ceremony as the official start of the post-study clock
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Begins preparing PGWP application documents as soon as final grades are submitted; submits the moment the letter of completion arrives by email
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: The 180-day window is fixed and non-extendable; every day of unnecessary delay is a day of lost buffer against processing complications, document issues, or IRCC delays

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Assumes that attending a DLI and graduating is sufficient for PGWP eligibility; never checks CIP codes or IRCC eligible fields lists
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Verifies program CIP code against IRCC's current eligible occupational fields list before starting the final year of study — and if studying post-November 2024, treats this as a go/no-go gate before investing further in the program
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Discovering ineligibility after graduation means losing both the tuition investment and the immigration pathway simultaneously, with no recourse

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Answers questionnaire questions based on intuitive logic (e.g., 'I have a job so I say yes to the job offer question'); uploads documents in whatever field seems closest in name
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Follows institution-provided step-by-step guides or consults an RCIC; knows that the portal's logic is counterintuitive and that specific answer combinations (like 'no' to job offer) are mandatory for correct routing
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: A single incorrect questionnaire answer can generate a different type of work permit than intended, requiring a full restart and additional fees while your work authorization timeline shrinks

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Books international travel home after graduation without checking TRV or eTA expiry; assumes pending application status allows free movement in and out of Canada
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Checks passport for TRV and eTA validity before making any travel plans post-submission; if either expires soon, delays travel until PGWP is approved or accepts consciously that re-entry may be delayed
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Being stranded outside Canada during PGWP processing can result in job loss, housing loss, missed academic or professional opportunities, and significant financial harm

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Starts working full-time the day after submitting the PGWP application, having heard that 'you can work while you wait,' without auditing personal compliance with the three qualifying conditions
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Audits own enrollment history and work-hour records against the three qualifying conditions before starting work; if any condition is uncertain, consults an RCIC before working
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Unauthorized work during a pending application creates an inadmissibility record that can permanently damage future permanent residence applications through Express Entry or PNP streams

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Assumes spouse will automatically receive an open work permit once PGWP is approved; plans family budget around two incomes without verifying TEER classification or permit remaining validity
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Researches the PGWP holder's NOC/TEER classification and expected job at time of application; calculates PGWP remaining validity at the point when the spouse would apply; stress-tests the family financial plan against a scenario where the spouse's OWP is not approved
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Post-January 2025 restrictions have significantly narrowed spousal OWP eligibility; families caught off guard by denial may face serious financial hardship with no immediate remedies
